metadata.title: JST and rJST: joint estimation of sentiment and topics in textual data using a semi-supervised approach

abstract:
This paper demonstrates the performance of the Joint Sentiment Topic model (JST) and the reversed Joint Sentiment Topic model (rJST) in measuring sentiment in political speeches, comparing them against a set of popular methods for sentiment analysis: widely used off-the-shelf sentiment dictionaries; an embeddings-enhanced dictionary approach; Latent Semantic Scaling, a semi-supervised approach; and a zero-shot transformer-based approach using a large language model (GPT-4). The findings reveal JST's superiority over all non-transformer-based approaches in predicting humancoded sentiment in multiple languages and its ability to replicate known sentiment trends in legislative speech. rJST, meanwhile, provides valuable topic-specific sentiment estimates, responsive to political dynamics and significant events. Both models are, however, outperformed by transformerbased models like GPT-4. Additionally, the paper introduces the 'sentitopics' R-package, designed to facilitate the use of JST and rJST in computational text analysis workflows. This package is compatible with popular text analysis tools, making the models accessible for applied researchers in communication science.

The widespread polarization of political competition in many countries has stimulated interest in automated sentiment analysis methods that seek to locate text documents on a positive-negative scale. sections.paper_id: Pipal et al. - 2025 - JST and rJST joint estimation of sentiment and topics in textual data using a semi-supervised appro
sections.header_raw: The advantages of jointly estimating sentiment and topics
sections.header_norm: the advantages of jointly estimating sentiment and topics
paragraphs: There exist numerous approaches to capturing sentiment in a text, such as manual coding, crowd coding, dictionary methods, supervised machine learning and deep learning approaches (for an overview, see van Atteveldt et al., 2021). Despite mixed performance, sentiment dictionaries such as LIWC (Tausczik & Pennebaker, 2010) or Lexicoder (Young & Soroka, 2012) are still popular because they are transparent, reliable, highly scalable, easy to use, and -once developed -their application is cost-free. A problem with general sentiment dictionaries, however, is that human language is domainspecific (Chan et al., 2021). For example, dictionaries that have been developed on a news article corpus have been found to perform better than general sentiment dictionaries in analyzing sentiment in news articles (Boukes et al., 2019;van Atteveldt et al., 2021;Young & Soroka, 2012). Rauh (2018) demonstrates that a sentiment dictionary tailored to German legislative rhetoric and party manifestos outperforms a generalized dictionary. These findings imply that tailored dictionaries are bound to perform better than off-the-shelf tools but the domain-specificity problem remains (Chan et al., 2021). Because even within the realm of, say, politics, the use of sentiment words is likely to be conditional on the topics under discussion. A politician who talks about national security is bound to use language that generally has a negative valence (e.g. words related to war) but not in the context of this topic. There is no general answer to how much of a problem such topic-specificity is in communication science research. But as we show in this paper, rJST specifically is a tool to examine its extent in specific applications, and both JST and rJST have procedures to describe sentiment while accounting for the issue of topic-specificity, allowing for more in-depth sentiment analysis.

JST and rJST have two further advantages. First, both models can easily be deployed in different languages. This is particularly useful because most computational text analysis tools are developed for and validated on English texts with non-English or multilingual tools that are comparatively rare (see Baden et al., 2022). Second, other procedures to generate topic-specific sentiment exist, but none with the speed and low costs of rJST and JST. For example, one could identify a topic per document or per sentence using human coding or by running a topic model. As a next step one could then use these texts to generate topic-specific sentiment relying on, for example, human crowd coding or dictionary expansion with word embeddings. Human coding is expensive and time-consuming. Word embeddings can be used to develop a sentiment dictionary from the ground up using a small set of seed words (Rheault et al., 2016), but the method requires large text corpora (e.g. the whole of Wikipedia or decades of parliamentary speeches) to find meaningful semantic relationships between words. Similarly, even though transformer-based models with transfer learning achieve state-of-the-art performance, they usually require extensive labeled data. Compared to these options, rJST and JST are faster (one-step procedure) and cheaper (no labeled input needed).

sections.paper_id: Pipal et al. - 2025 - JST and rJST joint estimation of sentiment and topics in textual data using a semi-supervised appro
sections.header_raw: The joint sentiment-topic and reverse joint sentiment-topic models
sections.header_norm: the joint sentiment topic and reverse joint sentiment topic models
paragraphs: JST and rJST were developed by researchers in computational linguistics who validated the models on the comparatively easy task of predicting sentiment in product or movie reviews (Lin & He, 2009;Lin et al., 2012). Such reviews commonly express a positive or negative opinion about a product, movie, or service (for a discussion, see Maks & Vossen, 2013). Texts such as political speeches and manifestos are far more complex and pose additional challenges to these models.

The key difference between JST and rJST concerns how they assume a text is generated. rJST assumes that speakers first choose a topic and then a sentiment to go with that topic. rJST thus treats a document as a mixture of topics first and a mixture of sentiments second. In contrast, JST assumes that speakers choose a sentiment first and a topic second (Lin & He, 2009;Lin et al., 2012). For political speech, one can easily think of examples of either sequence. For instance, a politician may be forced to focus on a topic because of constraints set by the legislative agenda or because of specific audience demands. In such cases, the speaker first chooses the topic, then the sentiment. But a speaker could also be motivated to convey a specific sentiment first. For example, at a rally a politician may want to spread enthusiasm among party activists, regardless of the topic. Both sets of assumptions are equally plausible. When choosing between JST and rJST, the decision should align with the specific research objectives. JST is preferable for tracking overall sentiment across texts, while rJST is more suitable for analyzing sentiment related to a specific topic within a text. This choice hinges on the nature of the research question rather than the inherent plausibility of each method's data-generating process assumptions.

Table 1 shows the generative processes of JST and rJST. We contrast these models with Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) (Blei et al., 2003). LDA is a topic model capable of clustering the content in large text corpora. It treats documents as mixtures of topics and estimates the prevalence of these topics as well as their content. JST and rJST both add a sentiment layer to this. While JST first samples words from a document-level sentiment distribution, rJST first samples them from a topic distribution, just like LDA. Then, for each topic, rJST chooses a sentiment distribution. In the third step, rJST assigns words to topics and sentiment. In contrast, JST first chooses a sentiment distribution, then chooses a topic distribution before assigning words to sentiment and topics.

paragraphs: JST and rJST models require minimal manual input, yet researchers must make crucial decisions regarding text preprocessing, supervised input selection, and model parameter configurations for optimal results.

Text preprocessing is the first critical step. Our analysis indicates that common preprocessing methods like stemming (reducing words to their base form) or trimming (eliminating infrequently used words) have a modest impact on model outcomes (see appendix B). Notably, trimming slightly decreases the correlation between JST sentiment estimates and human coding by 0.02 but only saves about 10% in computing time. Therefore, particularly for smaller datasets, we advise using an untrimmed corpus. For all of our subsequent analyses we applied the following preprocessings steps: lowercasing, removing numbers and punctuation, and stemming.

The second consideration involves selecting an appropriate dictionary for supervised input. Our validation demonstrates that using both general and context-specific dictionaries, • Choose a word wi from the distri- bution over words defined by the topic z i (parameter
• Choose a word w i from the distribu- tion over words defined by l i and z i (parameter ϕ l z i i )
(1) For each document d, choose a topic distribution
• Choose a word w i from the distri- bution over words defined by l i and z i (parameter ϕ l z i i ) Source: Blei et al. (2003), Lin andHe (2009), andLin et al. (2012).

including auto-translated versions, as supervised input produces results surpassing plain dictionary applications. One concern with dictionaries with high coverage (a large share of dictionary words present in the corpus) is that they leave too little room for the algorithm to find new associations between topics and sentiment. While Lin and He (2009) found the performance of the algorithm to improve when using a filtered dictionary (only keeping dictionary words appearing at least 50 times in the corpus), our models performed best when using the original dictionaries (see appendix B).

Third, the models require researchers to choose between estimating two or three sentiment categories. We found that choosing three categories (thus including a neutral category) often increased performance (see appendix B). Regarding algorithm iterations, rJST models in particular benefit from a higher number. We suggest a minimum of 1000 iterations for rJST. Hyperparameter tuning, especially the α parameter (dictating per-document sentiment-specific topic proportion), significantly influences model output. While JST models generally perform well with default settings, setting α to 0.15 improved coherence in rJST models in our application. Experimenting with different α values and closely reviewing each model's output is recommended. As with LDA models, estimating multiple models with varied settings and thoroughly examining the output and related speeches is essential for identifying the most effective configuration.

We strongly advise running each JST model multiple times and averaging the predictions from these iterations. Similar to LDA models, JST models do not give the exact same results across different runs (in the literature this is referred to as the issue of multi-modality (see Roberts et al. (2016)). Our validation procedures have demonstrated that averaging across multiple runs provides more reliable sentiment estimates. For document-level sentiment analysis, this approach also enables the calculation of uncertainty measures by considering the variation across these runs. While this can easily be done for JST models, this approach quickly becomes burdensome for rJST models as the topics have to be manually labeled.

Overall, to ensure the most accurate and relevant outcomes, we recommend that researchers meticulously review both the model output and the corresponding texts to identify the most appropriate settings for their specific application.

sections.paper_id: Pipal et al. - 2025 - JST and rJST joint estimation of sentiment and topics in textual data using a semi-supervised appro
sections.header_raw: Validating JST and rJST on political speech
sections.header_norm: validating jst and rjst on political speech
paragraphs: In this section, we examine whether JST and rJST provide valid estimates of sentiment expressed in political texts. We assess the concurrent validity of JST sentiment scores by comparing them with human-coded sentiment assessments of the same texts across three languages (English, German, and Dutch). This comparison extends to sentiment scores derived from sentiment dictionaries, embedding-enhanced dictionary expansion, Latent Semantic Scaling, and zero-shot transfer learning with GPT4. Additionally, we evaluate the predictive validity of JST by demonstrating its effectiveness in distinguishing between government and opposition stances. For rJST, we examine both face validity and discriminant validity by analyzing the model's capacity to categorize words into distinct sentiment categories across topics. 4 Additionally, we demonstrate its predictive validity, focusing on the dynamics of government-opposition sentiment within topics. Further, we evaluate the concurrent validity of EU topic-sentiment scores across three languages by comparing them with human-coded sentiment evaluations. This analysis includes a comparison with scores derived from sentiment dictionaries and zero-shot transfer learning with GPT4.

Further, we evaluate the concurrent validity of EU topic-sentiment scores across three languages by comparing them with human-coded sentiment evaluations. This analysis includes a comparison with scores derived from sentiment dictionaries and zero-shot transfer learning with GPT4.

sections.container_norm: validating speech level sentiment with jst
paragraphs: We start with a multi-language validation of JST with hand-coded speeches from the British House of Commons, the German Bundestag, and the Dutch Tweede Kamer. From the ParlSpeech v2 dataset (Rauh & Schwalbach, 2020), we sampled 200 speeches from each of these three parliaments, excluding speeches from the chair because those mainly concern parliamentary procedures and announce speaker turns. We recruited nine native speakers in total (3 per parliament/language) whom we asked to judge if a speech was broadly positive, neutral, or broadly negative. Each speech was coded by three coders. Since we were interested in the overall sentiment of the speech, we instructed coders to not focus on particular aspects of the speech, but to rate their overall impression of its sentiment. For this reason, we introduced an upper limit of 500 words per speech because for longer speeches, often covering multiple topics, this coding task would have been too hard. We also excluded speeches with fewer than 50 words because they carry little substantive content.

We follow Young and Soroka (2012) and Watanabe (2021) to convert the individual ratings from our three coders into a nuanced categorization. Each speech was placed on a 5-point scale, reflecting the overall sentiment. If all three coders agreed on a 'Negative' rating, the speech was classified as 'Very negative' (-2). A 'Somewhat negative' (-1) rating was assigned if two out of three coders marked the speech as 'Negative.' In cases where at least two coders agreed on a 'Neutral' rating or there was complete disagreement among the coders, the speech was designated as 'Neutral' (0). A speech was considered 'Somewhat positive' (+1) if two coders rated it as 'Positive,' and 'Very positive' (+2) if there was unanimous agreement on a 'Positive' rating. This method ensures a more precise and nuanced categorization of the speeches, capturing a spectrum of sentiments rather than a binary classification. Similar to the previous studies following this sentiment coding procedure (Watanabe, 2021;Young & Soroka, 2012), the agreement between coders is only moderately high (UK: Cohen's κ = 0.49; DE: Cohen's κ = 0.45; NL: Cohen's κ = 0.55). However, the disagreement between coders is attributed to their differing sensitivities to sentiment, a variation that we leverage to develop the 5-point scale.

In all three datasets, we observe a skewed distribution of sentiment labels with a majority of ratings falling into the negative or neutral category (UK: 29.1% Very negative, 18.1% Somewhat negative, 40.2% Neutral, 4.5% Somewhat positive, 8.0% Very positive; DE: 24.0% Very negative, 22.0% Somewhat negative, 43.5% Neutral, 4.5% Somewhat positive, 6.0% Very positive; NL: 29.6% Very negative, 14.1% Somewhat negative, 39.2% Neutral, 7.5% Somewhat positive, 9.5% Very positive). Such skewed distributions provide a challenge for machine learning algorithms, especially compared to curated and balanced training sets like movie reviews. Parliamentary speeches thus present a challenging test for the JST model.

sections.paper_id: Pipal et al. - 2025 - JST and rJST joint estimation of sentiment and topics in textual data using a semi-supervised appro
sections.container_norm: validating speech level sentiment with jst
paragraphs: To get JST estimates for these 200 speeches, we ran JST models with 10,000 randomly sampled speeches from each parliament (using the same criteria with which the 200 coded speeches were initially selected). Since JST is semi-supervised, it requires a sentiment dictionary as a seed dictionary. We compare JST performance when using general purpose and context-specific dictionaries as seed dictionaries. This includes the Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) dictionary (Tausczik & Pennebaker, 2010) as a general purpose dictionary, and the Lexicoder Sentiment dictionary (LSD) (Young & Soroka, 2012), originally developed for political news coverage, as a partly-context-specific dictionary. While both dictionaries were originally developed for and validated on English texts, human translated (LIWC) and auto-translated (LSD) (Proksch et al., 2019) versions are available in German and Dutch. We also include two domain-specific dictionaries developed for parliamentary speeches. For the speeches from the UK, we use the dictionary from Rheault et al. (2016), and for German speeches the dictionary developed by Rauh (2018). Due to the lack of sentiment dictionaries specifically developed for parliamentary speeches in Dutch, we only used the LIWC and LSD dictionaries for estimating JST models using Tweede Kamer speeches. When estimated, JST returns three separate probabilities for the sentiment labels neutral, positive, and negative. Since choosing an optimal number of topics a priori is difficult, we varied k between 5 and 30 in increments of 5 and estimated a JST model each time. Finally, we estimated each model 10 times and averaged their results. To arrive at a continuous sentiment measure per speech, we subtracted the score of the negative label from the score of the positive label. Thus, a speech with a JST estimate of 0.11 (Neutral), 0.65 (Positive), 0.24 (Negative) received an overall sentiment score of 0.41.

sections.container_norm: validating speech level sentiment with jst
paragraphs: To compare JST performance with dictionary applications, we also calculated sentiment measures using each dictionary alone (Lexicoder, LIWC, Rheault, Rauh). To this end, we counted the words identified by each dictionary category (positive and negative) in each speech and subtracted the negative matches from the positive matches.

To derive sentiment scores using expanded dictionaries, we use GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014) generated word vectors (300 dimensions and a 6-word context window), specifically trained on legislative speech data. The word vectors for English and German legislative speeches were provided by Rodriguez and Spirling (2022). Consequently, for the Dutch corpus, we trained a GloVe model locally. Using these word vectors, we evaluated the semantic proximity of all words in our texts to a predefined seed dictionary. For the seed dictionary, we used two sources: (a) a list of 14 polarity words from Turney and Littman (2003), and (b) a collection of 100 positive and 100 negative polarity words as identified in Rheault et al. (2016). We translated both word lists to German and Dutch for the respective corpora (the list of seed words can be found in appendix D). The aggregate sentiment for each text is then determined by calculating the average of the sentiment scores across the words that appear within that particular text.

We also calculate sentiment scores using Latent Semantic Scaling following the methodology outlined by Watanabe (2021). This process involves constructing the LSS model based on our sample of legislative speeches. Initially, we use the same set of 14 polarity seed words from Turney and Littman (2003), as used by Watanabe (2021), functioning as anchor points within the model. These anchor words guide the LSS model to generate a comprehensive sentiment dictionary, where each word is assigned a specific sentiment score. Subsequently, we analyze each text in our dataset, retrieving the corresponding sentiment score for every word. The overall sentiment of a text is then determined by averaging these individual word scores.

To classify speeches using transformer-based zero-shot transfer learning, we use OpenAI's API to prompt GPT-4 using the following instruction: "Your task is to evaluate the overall sentiment of a speech given in the [UK|German|Dutch] parliament. Rate the overall sentiment of the speech on a 5-point scale as as either Very negative, Somewhat negative, Neutral, Somewhat positive, or Very positive. [Answer with a sentiment label (either Very negative, Somewhat negative, Neutral, Somewhat positive, or Very positive). For instance: 'Very negative.' Do not use quotation marks.]". We then translated these labels into the same -2 to + 2 range to match the numeric human coding.

sections.paper_id: Pipal et al. - 2025 - JST and rJST joint estimation of sentiment and topics in textual data using a semi-supervised appro
sections.container_norm: validating speech level sentiment with jst
paragraphs: How does JST perform relative to the other methods? Table 2 presents the Pearson correlations between human-coded sentiment and (1) sentiment dictionaries, (2) JST estimates, (3) sentiment dictionaries built from GloVe word vectors and seed words, (4) Latent Semantic Scaling using seed words, and (5) zero-shot transfer learning with GPT-4. In all but one cases, the sentiment estimates obtained by JST have a higher correlation with human-coded sentiment than dictionary sentiment scores. It is only when we use the highly context-specific Rheault dictionary as seed dictionary that JST does not increase performance. Unexpectedly, the dictionary approach augmented with embeddings (GloVe) and Latent Semantic Scaling (LSS) yield inferior results compared to both the standard dictionary method and the JST approach. Conversely, in line with current research assessing the efficacy of Large Language Models (LLMs) in text classification tasks (Gilardi et al., 2023;Heseltine & Clemm von Hohenberg, 2024;Törnberg, 2023), GPT-4 surpasses all other methods, including JST. This is not surprising given the vast amount of semantic information embedded in transformer-based models, and LLMs in particular.

Overall, JST is always able to estimate sentiment better or equally well as dictionaries developed specifically for parliamentary rhetoric (Rheault and Rauh). This is exactly one of the goals of JST. All JST models consistently outperform context-free dictionaries (Lexicoder, LIWC). When no context-specific dictionary is available, our results indicate that for political speeches, across languages, the Lexicoder dictionaries provide the best results when used as the supervised input for JST models.

How much does choosing the "right" number of topics influence model performance? The performance does not fluctuate much between JST models with a different number of topics (mean range of r: 0.06, mean sd of r = 0.02). We provide a breakdown of this variation by corpus, dictionary, and topic numbers in appendix B. Therefore, guessing the "right" number of topics does not appear to be of much concern. Similar to our approach, analysts can always estimate several models within a reasonable range of topic numbers (e.g. by using LDA fit measures like perplexity and log-likelihood (Grün & Hornik, 2011) and average the results (like we do in this evaluation).

Unexpectedly, the dictionary approach augmented with embeddings (GloVe) and Latent Semantic Scaling (LSS) yield inferior results compared to both the standard dictionary method and the JST approach. Conversely, in line with current research assessing the efficacy of Large Language Models (LLMs) in text classification tasks (Gilardi et al., 2023;Heseltine & Clemm von Hohenberg, 2024;Törnberg, 2023), GPT-4 surpasses all other methods, including JST. This is not surprising given the vast amount of semantic information embedded in transformer-based models, and LLMs in particular.

Overall, JST is always able to estimate sentiment better or equally well as dictionaries developed specifically for parliamentary rhetoric (Rheault and Rauh). This is exactly one of the goals of JST. All JST models consistently outperform context-free dictionaries (Lexicoder, LIWC). When no context-specific dictionary is available, our results indicate that for political speeches, across languages, the Lexicoder dictionaries provide the best results when used as the supervised input for JST models.

How much does choosing the "right" number of topics influence model performance? The performance does not fluctuate much between JST models with a different number of topics (mean range of r: 0.06, mean sd of r = 0.02). We provide a breakdown of this variation by corpus, dictionary, and topic numbers in appendix B. Therefore, guessing the "right" number of topics does not appear to be of much concern. Similar to our approach, analysts can always estimate several models within a reasonable range of topic numbers (e.g. by using LDA fit measures like perplexity and log-likelihood (Grün & Hornik, 2011) and average the results (like we do in this evaluation).

paragraphs: To judge the predictive validity of JST, we probe the model's ability to replicate a consistent result from previous analyses on sentiment in legislative speech: the finding that government MPs use more positive sentiment than opposition MPs (Crabtree et al., 2020;Kosmidis et al., 2019;Osnabrügge et al., 2021;Pipal et al., 2024). To this end we again rely on the ParlSpeech v2 corpus examining all speeches from the House of Commons between 1988 and 2020 (1,956,223 speeches, 2,175 speakers), the Bundestag between 1991 and 2018 (379,545 speeches, 2,374 speakers), and the Tweede Kamer between 1994 and 2019 (1,143,366 speeches, 1,209 speakers). We aggregated all speeches a speaker delivered in a quarter of a year, and estimated JST models using these speaker-quarter observations. We estimated JST models using the Lexicoder sentiment dictionaries as input and topic numbers (UK: 180 topics, DE: 120 topics, NL: 190 topics) based on LDA fit measures.

Figure 1 displays the estimated JST sentiment scores aggregated by year and party in the three studied legislatures for two selected parties with changing government status. Overall, JST replicates the consistent findings in the literature that government politicians speak more positively than opposition politicians. In each legislature, when a party joins the government, its MPs speak more positively. Vice versa, when it leaves the government, its MPs speak more negatively. 2018) is only available for the English and German corpora, respectively. Bold values indicate the higher correlation (JST vs. dictionary) and best correlation (GloVe, LSS, GPT-4).

paragraphs: To evaluate the validity of topic-sentiment with rJST we first look in depth at face validity, discriminant validity, and predictive validity when interpreting topic-sentiment words using legislative speeches from the United Kingdom. In addition, we evaluate concurrent validity with human-coded data across all three corpora. For all validations, we use a 10% sample of each corpus to speed up computing time. We estimated multiple rJST models with a varying number of topics and followed the preprocessing steps described in the previous section, again using the Lexicoder sentiment dictionary as supervised input.

We scrutinized the estimated topics qualitatively by inspecting the words highly associated with each topic-sentiment and by reading speeches that scored high on those. Using this procedure, we decided on models with 100 topics. In appendix E, we demonstrate that rJST works also with smaller corpora and lower topic numbers using the EUSpeech v2 dataset of speeches by European political leaders (Schumacher et al., 2020).

paragraphs: To illustrate the rJST output we highlight two topics from the English corpus: armed forces/security and the European Union. Table 3 shows word stems that score highest on the three sentiment categories for each of these two topics. Most words appear in the category one would expect based on general sentiment dictionaries. However, stems like "forc" (positive) "royal" (negative) or "war" (neutral) are in a category that makes sense in the context of war. Importantly, these categories are different from those in the LSD dictionary, where "forc" is scored negative, "royal" positive, and "war" negative. Similarly, for the European Union topic, "agreement" and "relationship" are identified as negative by rJST but are likely to be classified as positive by dictionaries (e.g. "agreement" is scored positive in the NRC dictionary). Many positive stems for the European Union topic refer to countries (e.g. "germani," "french," "britain"). This is a result of MPs emphasizing the strong, positive relations with these countries.

Placing these context-dependent words in their proper sentiment category is exactly the goal of rJST. 5 But how context-dependent are these sentiment categories exactly? To answer this question, we correlated the rJST topic-sentiment scores to the sentiment scores we would have obtained by just using the Lexicoder sentiment dictionary. For speeches covering the topic of Armed Forces/ Security, the Pearson's correlation is r = 0.05. For speeches covering the EU topic, the Pearson's correlation is r = -0.04. This shows that rJST and sentiment dictionaries can produce very different substantive results.

To further illustrate the value of the rJST model, consider the first two speeches in Table 4. The first speech was delivered by Theresa May and concerns the terror attacks in Paris and the readiness of the United Kingdom to counter such threats. The dictionary scores this passage as negative, as a consequence of words such as "attacks," "casualties," and "terrorists." rJST, however, gives it a positive score because May praises the government's readiness in the case of terrorist attacks. Within the context of the topic of Armed Forces/security, it makes a lot of sense to consider this speech to be positive. In the second speech, Dominic Raab talks about the UK leaving the European Union. He does not use any negative words, which results in a positive sentiment dictionary score. From our domain knowledge about the European affairs, it is, however, evident that Raab talks negatively about the EU. The rJST model has picked up on these domain differences and provides a negative score for this speech. Of course, we do not observe such differences for all speeches. For instance, the estimated sentiments using rJST and the plain dictionary are much closer to the speeches by Gordon Brown and Priti Patel in Table 4. Here, both methods give similar results. 5 Not all topics are as consistent as the topics above. Comparable to LDA topic models, some topics are just so-called overflow topics.

To sum up, we have given several examples of rJST picking up sentiment specific to a topic, where dictionary approaches fail to account for such context-specificity. An added benefit of rJST is that for longer speeches that contain various topics, the procedure can distinguish between the sentiment on these topics. In contrast, a dictionary procedure can only produce an overall score. Getting topic-specific sentiment scores using a dictionary would require a multi-step procedure. [..] Of course, we have long had detailed plans for dealing with these kinds of attacks. The House will recall the attacks in Mumbai in 2008 when terrorists armed with assault weapons and explosives took the lives of more than 150 people. Since 2010, and learning the lessons of that attack, we have improved our police firearms capability and the speed of our military response, and we have enhanced protective security where possible through a range of other measures. We have improved joint working between the emergency services to deal specifically with marauding gun attacks. Specialist joint police, ambulance and fire teams are now in place in key areas across England, with equivalents in Scotland and Wales, and they are trained and equipped to manage casualties in the event of that kind of an attack. The police and other agencies regularly carry out exercises to test the response to a terrorist attack, and these exercises include scenarios that are similar to the events in Paris. We will ensure that future exercises reflect specific elements of the Paris attacks, so we can learn from them and be ready for them should they ever occur in the United Kingdom. [..] (Topic: Armed Forces/Security)

The referendum that we saw in 2016 was a brilliant example of a thriving democracy. That vote, whether it had been to leave or to remain, although the majority vote was to leave, was a vote of confidence in our democratic process. It was a vote of confidence in Britain, and it is incumbent on all of us to respect that result and deliver that outcome.

The position respects the vote of the people in the referendum of 2016 to leave the EU. It also reflects the will of Parliament. The Government have successfully triggered article 50, pursuant to an Act of Parliament passed last year in the Commons on Second Reading by 498 votes to 114-an overwhelming majority-and they are negotiating for a good outcome that works for both the people and businesses in the UK and those in the EU. As someone who campaigned to leave the European Union, I understand that those who signed the petition are impatient to leave the EU and are asking the Government to leave the negotiations before 2019.[..] (Topic: EU) rJST: -1.39 Dictionary: 1.75 Distance: 3.14 G. Their bravery and the sacrifice they have made for the future of Afghanistan and for the security of the British people will not be forgotten. Our thoughts today are with their families and loved ones as they receive this very sad news. I am sure that the House will also want us to pay respects to Dr. Ashok Kumar, who sadly died this week. sections.paper_id: Pipal et al. - 2025 - JST and rJST joint estimation of sentiment and topics in textual data using a semi-supervised appro
sections.container_norm: validating speech level sentiment with jst
paragraphs: To what extent is rJST capable of discriminating between words that are always negative or positive, or are sometimes positive and sometimes negative? To answer this question, using again the English example, we selected the 100 most strongly associated words per sentiment-topic combination, relying on the rJST model that we introduced in the previous section. Per unique word we then created a list of the senti-topics with which that word is associated. Among these 30,000 words (100 topics * 3 sentiment categories * 100 most strongly associated words), there are 10,337 unique words. There are fewer unique words than there are total words, since many words carrying sentiment are used across topics. Out of these 10,337 words, 2,343 (22.6%) are strictly associated with neutral sentiment categories. About 40% of the remaining words are only negative and 30% are only positive, while about 30% of words occur in the top 100 most strongly associated words of both negative and positive senti-topics. It is these words we are most interested in, because they show where topics and sentiment have an influence on each other, demonstrating the flexibility of the rJST model. We illustrate this using a selection of example words.

The word "difficulti(es)," for instance, is commonly perceived as negative, but rJST places it in five positive and four negative senti-topics. Depending on the topic, it is used to describe difficulties or, contrasting, to solving or dealing with difficult situations. For instance, it is used negatively in the context of drugs/substance abuse, but positively in the topic about insurance/finances. "Unemploy(ed/ ment)", on the other hand, is used almost exclusively in a negative context, for instance when talking about business/privatizations. However, on the issue of jobs/unemployment itself it is also indicative of positive sentiment. This is because in this context, it is often used in connection to "fighting unemployment" or other proposals to reduce unemployment. In contrast, the Lexicoder sentiment dictionary always treats the word as negative. The word "love" is similar in this regard. Except for one topic it is always used in a positive senti-topic. But when talking about crime/suffering, "love" is put in the negative sentiment category. This is because the bi-gram "loved ones"-commonly used to describe the loss of someone -is reduced to the uni-grams "love" and "one" during preprocessing. Nevertheless, rJST is able to identify the contexts the word is used in. The Lexicoder sentiment dictionary, in comparison, always counts the occurrence of the word "love" as positive.

Overall, every dictionary can only treat these words as either positive or negative, or ignore that they carry sentiment in some contexts entirely. From the 2,265 words that appear in both positive and negative rJST senti-topics, 143 appear in the negative word list of the Lexicoder sentiment dictionary, and 179 in the positive word list. Put differently, 6.04% (13.49%) of the words in the negative (positive) Lexicoder word list appear at least once in a rJST senti-topic with opposite polarity. Examples include words such as "obstacle" (Lexicoder: negative, rJST: 66% positive), "mean" (Lexicoder: negative, rJST: 22% positive), and "peace" (Lexicoder: positive, rJST: 66% negative).

sections.paper_id: Pipal et al. - 2025 - JST and rJST joint estimation of sentiment and topics in textual data using a semi-supervised appro
sections.container_norm: validating speech level sentiment with jst
paragraphs: To probe the predictive validity of rJST and demonstrate its full output, we investigate topic prevalence and topic sentiment in legislative speeches from the UK. Similar to our discussion of JST, we expect to see (1) an opposition/government dynamic, with MPs from the latter being generally more positive, and (2) responsiveness to high-profile events. We show the same validation using legislative speeches from Germany and the Netherlands in appendix C.

Figure 2 shows quarterly average topic prevalence and topic-sentiment for four selected topics over time. We use the same rJST model as in previous sections. The upper panel A shows the quarterly topic prevalence of four selected topics: Armed Forces/Security, Health care/NHS, Economy/Financial Crisis, and Europe/EU. In line with what we would expect, we see an increased salience of military and security issues as a topic after the 9/11 terror attacks. Similarly, the economic/financial crisis as a topic was most prominent during the great financial crisis following the Lehman Brothers crash in 2008. Responsiveness is most evident when considering the European Union as a topic: its salience increased already before the 2016 Brexit referendum, and increased even more sharply after the vote to leave the EU. The lower panels B (armed forces/security), C (healthcare/NHS), D (economy/financial crisis), and E (Europe/EU) display the respective quarterly aggregated topic-sentiment.

Turning to topic-sentiment, in three of four topics we replicate the government-opposition dynamics we observe when plotting JST sentiment scores: politicians from the government party speak more positively than opposition candidates, also about specific topics. This is, however, not the case for the issue of the European Union, where for the most time Labour MPs speak more positively than Conservative MPs. In addition, following the Brexit referendum of 2016, politicians of both parties spoke increasingly negative about the EU with an, as expected, disappearing governmentopposition effect. These results show that rJST can help us examine government-opposition dynamics in more depth: opposition speakers are comparatively more negative than government speakers on some topics but not on other topics.

sections.container_norm: validating speech level sentiment with jst
paragraphs: For our comparison with human-coded sentiment, we sampled 200 speeches that rJST scored higher than 0.10 on EU-topic prevalence from each of the three parliaments. We recruited three research assistants, who, along with one of the authors, first determined whether a speech was indeed focused on the EU/European politics topic. Following this initial assessment (UK: 178 speeches, DE: 172 speeches, NL: 160 speeches), they then judged if a speech was broadly positive, neutral, or broadly negative about the EU/European politics. Each speech was coded by three coders. Because this time we were interested in the topic-specific sentiment of the speech, we instructed coders to focus on those aspects of the speech that cover the EU or European politics. We then followed the same procedure as before to construct a 5-point (Very negative (-2) to Very positive (+2)) topic-sentiment scale. Again, the agreement between the coders is moderately high (UK: Cohen's κ = 0.44; NL: Cohen's κ = 0.52) to substantial (DE: Cohen's κ = 0.71), capturing their differing sensitivities to sentiment. In each of the three datasets, we again observe a skewed distribution of sentiment labels (UK: 24.7% Very negative, 17.5% Somewhat negative, 35.5% Neutral, 22.3% Somewhat positive; DE: 19.9% Very negative, 4.8% Somewhat negative, 25.3% Neutral, 50.0% Somewhat positive; NL: 28.5% Very negative, 13.1% Somewhat negative, 41.5% Neutral, 16.9% Somewhat positive). Notably, across all languages, no speech was classified as "Very Positive."

sections.container_norm: validating speech level sentiment with jst
paragraphs: To compare the topic-sentiment scores obtained from rJST to those based on the dictionary alone, we use the Lexicoder dictionary to score the sentiment of the EU-focused speeches. Because the sentiment dictionary does not distinguish between topics, this measure equates overall speech sentiment with EU topic-specific sentiment. We follow the same procedures as before for the computation of sentiment scores using embedding enhanced dictionary expansion with GloVe word vectors and Latent Semantic Scaling. Similarly to the dictionary measures, these scores are obtained for the entire document, and thus also equate overall speech sentiment with EU topic-specific sentiment.

We further again use OpenAI's API to prompt GPT-4, but use an adjusted instruction: "Your task is to evaluate the sentiment towards the topic 'EU/European politics' in a speech given in the (UK/German/ Dutch) parliament. Rate the sentiment of the speech towards this topic as either Very Negative, Somewhat Negative, Neutral, Somewhat Positive, or Positive. Remember that a speech might be generally positive while expressing negative sentiments towards the EU/European politics, or vice versa. [Answer with a sentiment label (either Very negative, Somewhat negative, Neutral, Somewhat positive, or Very positive). For instance: 'Very negative.' Do not use quotation marks.]". We then translated these labels into the same -2 to + 2 range to match the numeric human coding.

Rate the sentiment of the speech towards this topic as either Very Negative, Somewhat Negative, Neutral, Somewhat Positive, or Positive. Remember that a speech might be generally positive while expressing negative sentiments towards the EU/European politics, or vice versa. [Answer with a sentiment label (either Very negative, Somewhat negative, Neutral, Somewhat positive, or Very positive). For instance: 'Very negative.' Do not use quotation marks.]". We then translated these labels into the same -2 to + 2 range to match the numeric human coding.
sections.preview_head: To compare the topic-sentiment scores obtained from rJST to those based on the dictionary alone, we use the Lexicoder dictionary to score the sentiment of the EU-focused speeches. Because the sentiment dictionary does not distinguish between topics, this measure equates overall speech sentiment with EU topic-specific sentiment. We follow the same procedures as before for the computation of sentiment scores using embedding enhanced dictionary expansion with GloVe word vectors and Latent Semantic Scaling.

paragraphs: Table 5 displays the Pearson correlations between the automated measures and the human coding. The results indicate a generally poor performance for both sentiment dictionaries and rJST. While the correlations obtained through rJST are higher than those obtained through sentiment dictionaries for the UK and Dutch corpora, they remain substantially low. In contrast, in the German corpus, the dictionary clearly outperforms rJST, displaying a relatively high correlation with human coding. This is contradictory to our earlier assessment of rJST, which showed promising outcomes, such as coherent topic-sentiment words and credible temporal trends. However, rJST falls short in accurately predicting the topic-specific sentiment of individual speeches. In contrast, the application of zero-shot transfer learning with GPT-4 shows markedly superior results, demonstrating robust capability in estimating topic-specific sentiment across different languages.

paragraphs: In this paper, we set out to evaluate the validity and utility of estimating sentiment and topics simultaneously in political text using JST/rJST. Our findings underscore the added value that both models have for communication-and social scientists who want to conduct sentiment analysis on textual data. First, we have shown that sentiment scores estimated from JST are, in almost all cases we explored, better predictive of human-coded sentiment of legislative speeches than sentiment dictionaries. JST outperformed both generalized and context-specific sentiment dictionaries on this metric in English, Dutch and German speeches. Additionally, JST outperforms scores obtained through embedding-enhanced dictionary expansion and Latent Semantic Scaling. JST cannot, however, keep up with the performance of state-of-the-art transformer-based transfer learning using GPT-4. This is not surprising given the vast amount of semantic information embedded in these models. Second, we show that rJST allows for fast estimation of topicspecific sentiment. Not only is this a useful quantity of interest for many communication science applications, it could also serve as a tool for constructing topic-specific sentiment dictionaries. Substantively, we demonstrated that rJST topic-sentiments track widely documented government-opposition dynamics and respond in expected ways to real-world events. However, when rJST is assessed against human-coded evaluations of topic-specific sentiment at the speech level, its performance is relatively underwhelming. Although it demonstrates higher correlations with human coding compared to plain dictionary applications in two out of three corpora, these correlations are still substantially low. Again, both rJST and dictionaries are clearly outperformed by transformer-based transfer learning using GPT-4.

Our performance evaluation builds on a moderately high level of agreement between coders on the (topic-)sentiment of political speeches. This modest degree of coder agreement aligns with findings from previous studies, including those by Watanabe (2021) and Young and Soroka (2012), and may be Bold values indicate the higher correlation (JST vs. dictionary) and best correlation (GloVe, LSS, GPT-4).

considered a form of "valid disagreement" as conceptualized by Baden et al. (2023). Such discrepancies between coders reflect the subjective nature of interpreting sentiment in political texts, suggesting that the "ground truth" in this context comprises varied, yet significant, human judgments. For any application, when using and validating JST/rJST it is crucial to carefully consider the ground truth that the model is tested against. Although outperformed by newer transformer-based models, the significance of lightweight models extends beyond mere analytical performance, and we believe that both JST and rJST hold significant value as analytical tools for communication scholars working with textual data. First, the environmental and financial costs associated with deploying heavyweight models such as GPT-4 should not be overlooked. As highlighted by Bender et al. (2021), the computational and energy demands of training and running LLMs present substantial environmental implications, reinforcing the relevance of more sustainable, low-resource alternatives. Environmental concerns aside, we should also not underestimate the continued relevance and advantages of simpler, established methods, as they often offer transparency and interpretability -attributes that may be diminished or lost in more complex models. For instance, the semi-supervised nature of JST and rJST allows researchers to integrate their domain knowledge directly during the model-building phase, an advantage not readily available in newer architectures. Second, rJST can serve as a tool for longitudinal analysis, providing topic-specific sentiment insights. This feature is invaluable for exploratory analyses of trends over time, such as tracking the evolution of policy discussions in response to events or throughout legislative periods. When the research focus is on broader trends or thematic structures rather than precise accuracy at the individual text level, rJST offers a valuable analytical approach.

Moreover, JST and rJST can be seen as exploratory, hypothesis-generating instruments. They allow for the extraction of broad insights from a dataset, which can then be explored in more detail using targeted measurement techniques. Despite their limitations, these models contribute to scholarly discussions on sentiment analysis in political texts, encouraging further methodological and theoretical development.
Additionally, the availability of these models in an R package simplifies their extension and adaptation.

In conclusion, in this paper we have demonstrated the usefulness of JST and rJST for the analysis of sentiment in political texts. However, the importance of methodological validation remains critical (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013), requiring extensive validation by the analyst who cannot escape from close reading of key texts to ensure that model results are indeed reliable and valid.

In this paper, we set out to evaluate the validity and utility of estimating sentiment and topics simultaneously in political text using JST/rJST. Our findings underscore the added value that both models have for communication-and social scientists who want to conduct sentiment analysis on textual data. First, we have shown that sentiment scores estimated from JST are, in almost all cases we explored, better predictive of human-coded sentiment of legislative speeches than sentiment dictionaries. JST outperformed both generalized and context-specific sentiment dictionaries on this metric in English, Dutch and German speeches. Additionally, JST outperforms scores obtained through embedding-enhanced dictionary expansion and Latent Semantic Scaling. JST cannot, however, keep up with the performance of state-of-the-art transformer-based transfer learning using GPT-4. This is not surprising given the vast amount of semantic information embedded in these models. Second, we show that rJST allows for fast estimation of topicspecific sentiment. Not only is this a useful quantity of interest for many communication science applications, it could also serve as a tool for constructing topic-specific sentiment dictionaries. Substantively, we demonstrated that rJST topic-sentiments track widely documented government-opposition dynamics and respond in expected ways to real-world events. However, when rJST is assessed against human-coded evaluations of topic-specific sentiment at the speech level, its performance is relatively underwhelming. Although it demonstrates higher correlations with human coding compared to plain dictionary applications in two out of three corpora, these correlations are still substantially low. Again, both rJST and dictionaries are clearly outperformed by transformer-based transfer learning using GPT-4.

Our performance evaluation builds on a moderately high level of agreement between coders on the (topic-)sentiment of political speeches. paragraphs: Christian Pipal is a senior research and teaching associate (Oberassistent) in the Department of Communication and Media Research at the University of Zurich. His research focuses on political communication on digital platforms, political influencers, and the role of emotions in political communication. This research was part of his PhD trajectory in the Hot Politics Lab at the University of Amsterdam.

Martijn Schoonvelde is an assistant professor of European politics and society at the University of Groningen. His research focuses on political rhetoric, computational text analysis, and European politics.

Gijs Schumacher is an associate professor in the Department of Political Science at the University of Amsterdam and codirector of the Hot Politics Lab. The Hot Politics Lab is an interdisciplinary research group at the University of Amsterdam that analyzes the role of emotions, personality, and language in politics.
Max Boiten is a course instructor at Utrecht University.
